data.title: Hedging The Dollar: Bitcoin, Gold, Or What?
data.description:
The U.S. dollar has been falling. Gold and Bitcoin have been rising. Which will protect the U.S. dollar in your portfolio? Or should it be something else? What about the U.S. dollar index, or just the Eurodollar currency, or even Coinbase (the crypto exchange) or the S&P?

I’m going to use BTC-USD data from 2017, when bitcoin futures (BTC) started trading, and because more recent volatility is important. But all the data will be cash prices, not back-adjusted futures, except for Coinbase (COIN) and SPY (the S&P ETF). Gold is physical gold prices, and DX is the futures symbol for the dollar index.

The motivation for this is whether gold or bitcoin is a better investment. Perhaps you don’t need my analysis to make your decision, but there are a few points that you should consider.

  1. Gold collapsed after 1980, and it took 25 years before it moved up. It could decline again and stay down. Gold is not normally volatile, but the recent run-up makes it more vulnerable.
  2. BTC-USD has no intrinsic value, nor does any of the cryptocurrencies. Its price is entirely based on demand, or lack of demand. It can be volatile.
  3. Which of the two will protect against losses in the U.S. dollar? Or, does it matter?
  4. Which offers the best diversification to an S&P portfolio?
  5. Is it better to trade the exchange, COIN, than BTC-USD?

History of Gold Prices

I was trading in 1980 when gold peaked along with silver. It coincided with 18% interest rates, something I hope won’t happen again. It then languished for 30 years until just after the 2008 financial crisis. In Chart 1, it certainly looks different now.

Chart 1. Physical gold prices from 1978 through October 21, 2025. (Source: Commodity Systems Inc. data)

Note that having reached nearly $2,000, it retracted to $1,000, a 50% decline. A reminder that nothing is without risk.

History of Bitcoin Prices

Realistically, Bitcoin only goes back to 2017, when futures began trading, giving us about 8 years of data. BTC-USD has always been volatile and is not likely to change. Chart 2 shows both physical gold (on the right scale) and BTC-USD (on the left scale), both adjusted to 100 on 1/2/2017. A first glance shows the remarkable return of BTC-USD and the low volatility of gold.

Chart 2. BTC-USD and physical gold cash prices. (Source: Commodity Systems Inc. data)

Hedging the U.S. Dollar

Gold has always been seen as a hedge against a declining U.S. dollar. BTC-USD originally advertised itself as a “currency,” but it’s not clear just what it is.

The dollar gains and loses based on the U.S. economy. When investors buy U.S. treasuries or buy U.S. stocks, they convert their currency to U.S. dollars. When we sell wheat or soybeans for export, we increase the value of the dollar.

The opposite can happen. If investors are not happy with U.S. policies, they abandon the dollar, they don’t buy U.S. grains, they pull their investments out of the stock markets. The dollar declines. That has happened recently.

This is a good time to see whether gold, BTC-USD, or some other market can offset the loss in the dollar. First, let’s look at the U.S. Dollar Index (DX futures, underlying cash value), a composite of major currencies. When under 100, the dollar is weak. When above 100, it is strong. In general, the dollar has held in a neutral range but is clearly weaker than it was in late 2022.

Chart 3. The dollar index, underlying futures cash prices (DX-ICE).

But perhaps looking only at the euro currency may be easier to see. The way the price is read is “how many USD per euro.” When the price rises, the dollar weakens. In Chart 4, the weakest U.S. dollar value was 125. It is now between 115 and 120, on the weaker side.

Chart 4. EURUSD dollar cash prices. (Source: Data from Commodity Systems Inc)

The charts show that the dollar has been here before, but some investors want to protect against further losses. What is the best market?

Correlations

Correlations can tell you a lot. We calculate the daily returns of each cash price, then use the Excel function CORREL to create a cross-correlation table from 2017, shown in Chart 5. I’ve included COIN, the exchange that trades cryptocurrencies, and SPY, just in case!

Chart 5. Correlating gold and BTC-USD with the Euro currency and the Dollar Index. (Source: Data from Commodity System, Inc).

We first want to look at what markets are negatively correlated to the dollar index. We see the EURUSD, but prices are the reverse of DX, so that doesn’t count. But gold and the dollar index have a negative correlation. When gold goes up, the dollar index drops. That’s what we were looking for.

On the other hand, BTC-USD has the same relationship, but much weaker (-0.100 versus -0.346). In fact, we see that all of the markets are negatively correlated to DX. Note that, from a statistical viewpoint, small positive and negative correlations don’t mean much. The most important ones are gold-DX and gold-EURUSD. COIN vs SPY are positively correlated, no doubt because both have gone higher for different reasons.

Returns and Risk

Correlations are important, but we need to know about the returns and risk. Chart 6 shows both, plus the ratio of return to risk, which tells us which is the best payout. Bitcoin clearly has the best return, but at a risk that is most likely to wipe you out.

Chart 6. Annualized returns and annualized risk. (Source: Data from Commodity Systems Inc)

Gold turns out to be fairly stable, with the returns slightly better than the risk. Given that it is negatively correlated to the S&P (SPY), it is my choice for the best way to hedge the dollar. The other markets have very low returns; consequently, a lower ratio.

Combining a portfolio of SPY and physical gold seems ideal. But again, it is not without risk. Gold and the SPY can both decline at the same time, even if the long-term forecast looks promising. Given the choices, it is still the best.

data.title: I Bonds Look Attractive Right Now Amid Uncertainty: Buy Before November 1 Reset
data.description:
The BLS reported at 8:30 EST today that the 6-month annualized rate for September was 3.12% which combined with a fixed rate of 1.10% produces a total 6-month return of 4.22% for I Bonds purchased before the November 1 reset. Combined with the preceding 6-month total return of 3.98% the total return for the full 12 months will be 4.10%. A broad outline of implications follows.

In previous articles I have written about an unusual advantage an I Bond investor has in the days before the May 1 or November 1 reset. If you buy now, in advance of the November 1 reset, you will get the current fixed rate of 1.1% plus the variable (inflation adjusted) rate from November 1, 2024 through September 2025. Think about it. It's as if you were able to buy a stock or an index at its price a full year in the past with the one year return guaranteed. Who could say no to that advantage? If you don't like that return, you do nothing. If you like it, you pile in and buy as much as the allowable $10,000 and possibly more using the Gift Box.

Of course bond returns are minuscule compared to stock returns, but if you want to own any super-safe bonds, the current I Bond is a good option. It's particularly good as fixed rates are likely to change on November 1 in an unfavorable direction, most likely falling from 1.1% to 0.9%. Fixed income yields in general are more likely than not to fall as the Federal Reserve, under pressure from the President, cuts rates this year and probably continues well into next year. While it is always possible that rates at the longer end of the yield curve may stay as they are or even go up a bit it seems likely that rates on straight (non inflation adjusted) Treasury Notes up to 5 years will go down. There is consequently a likelihood that other sorts of fixed income such as CDs will follow.

If you want to play this, you should buy your desired quantity of I Bonds up to $10,000 as soon as possible, ideally early next week, so as not to get bumped beyond November 1. If you want to buy in a big way, you should take a last opportunity to make a pair with a trusted friend or family member and buy on a larger scale using the Gift Box. I have done this in the past and might do it again this year if I find an interested partner. But as with your own purchase you should do it well in advance of November 1, ideally by October 28, next Tuesday. That should give you enough time to think it over. Although you can still buy both your personal limit and the Gift Box amount later in the year after the reset, the fixed rate will likely be that 0.9% mentioned above. Students of I Bond fixed rates such as Dave Enna of Tipswatch have observed that since 2017 the I Bond fixed rate has been 65% of the real yield of the 5-year Treasury and the current calculation is so close to 0.95% that a minor shift may have nudged it either way, toward 0.9% or 1.0%. These sound like insignificant numbers but as the fixed rate on a current purchase will continue for the next 30 years that seemingly insignificant number adds up quite a bit. So buy now if you can or kick yourself later.

I should mention here that after the end of the year there will likely be major changes in the Gift Box and TreasuryDirect is "requesting" that Gift Box participants have their Gift Box I Bonds "delivered" by the end of the year. I myself and all Gift Box participants received a message from TreasuryDirect with this request, starting with the line "Treasury is always looking for ways to enhance your experience." That may well be true but the phone answerers at TreasuryDirect have a history of being somewhat disorganized in the face of major changes - this was especially so during the recent temporary but steep rise in inflation from 2021 through 2023, peaking at 9.1% in the middle of 2022 - and Treasury Direct was caught off guard both in their ability to fill purchases in a timely fashion and having not originally thought through the Gift Box and what impact major events might have on its use. As a result they must "suggest" actions that they can't really enforce. I am nevertheless planning to get together with my I Bond Gift Box partner and deliver our Gift I Bonds to each other.

Details Alluded To In The Above Paragraphs With Details on How I Bonds Work to Follow

The present government shutdown produced a one-week delay, the Bureau of Labor Statistics assured that the data used was accurate and based on the same statistical measures as usual. If you buy an I Bond before November 1 (remember: try to be a few days early), you will receive the variable rate for November 1, 2024, through April 30, 2025, plus the variable rate from May 1, 2025, through September 30, 2025. To state the obvious, the last month included is not the current month (October) but the last month for which full data is available (September). A favorable quirk in the TreasuryDirect I Bond rules is that I Bonds purchase at any time of the month will receive interest for the full month. Purchasing now therefore gets a little extra in return.

As mentioned earlier the fixed rate for both six month periods is 1.1% and a major reason to buy now is that the fixed rate starting November 1 is certain to decline. The variable rate from last November 1 through April 30, 2025, was 2.86% so that adding the fixed rate of 1.10% the overall return for the first six-month period was 3.98%. With today's CPI report we now know that the variable rate for May 1 through September 30 will be 3.12% and adding in the 1.10% fixed rate produces a 4.22% number for the just ended six months.

Averaging the two numbers produces a number of 4.10% return for the entire year before adding in the relatively small compounding. In any case that's an excellent number for a flexible asset which you can get out of in a year and three months by sacrificing one quarter (three months) of returns. If interested in short term return but flexible, you might wait to choose a three-month period with low inflation and low variable returns. It substantially beats the yield of any other super-safe asset for a year and a quarter. It gets better from there as one can depart after five years with no sacrifice. You can then consider year to year for the next 30 years whether it remains a good deal. For the record I have never cashed in an I Bond and don't intend to in the future.

At the present moment most analysts who bother to think about it seem to feel that inflation will likely remain about the same as the present to the distant event horizon. Today's Big Money poll in Barron's did exactly that, with the majority expecting 3% as far as the eye can see. Your response to that will depend upon your point of view. I'll be long gone in 30 years (being 81) but my daughter who is the designated heir for my I Bonds will likely be in her early 80s. I have explained to her how I Bonds worked and given her the cash to buy an I Bond on her own, a task that she performed successfully. A lot can happen in 30 years, especially when it comes to inflation. If your need is "inflation insurance," you would do well to consider the amount of U.S. government debt which is likely to be engaged by some form of money printing which will bring with it major inflation. I don't actually see my I Bond portfolio as insurance that you can cash and take to the grocery store, but more as an asset class you will be happy to have as part of your total portfolio if major inflation takes place. I have purchased I Bonds regularly since the year 2000 and they have performed spectacularly to that goal.

The present moment has more than the normal degree of uncertainty. President Trump's policies could produce anything from inflation to deflation, maybe one after the other, and I Bonds have a wonderful quality talked about in the final section below. They are structured to protect you against both inflation and deflation.

For what it's worth the 100-plus years of good records show that inflation has a very long term average of 3% although that 3% average puts together a lumpy and bumpy history running from the extreme deflation of the 1930s to the extreme inflation of the 1970s which I myself experienced. I will continue to buy I Bonds as long as they are offered. The ones I was fortunate to buy starting in 2000 have amounted to a sum I won't boast about. I actually bought them casually, almost on a whim at a time when the stock market looked awful and overpriced and soon crashed spectacularly. So buy I Bonds by early next week if they meet your personal goals.

In writing about I Bonds I assume that the majority of my readers are already well informed about the way they work. On the other hand, a few will be new to I Bonds so I always include some form of the following section giving a sort of beginner's version on how I Bonds work and how they resemble and differ from similar assets. Even if you have known about I Bonds for a long time you may want to refresh your memory in case you have forgotten some details. I do so myself whenever writing a new article on I Bonds.

What Are I Bonds And How Do They Work?

I Bonds are a form of US Treasury Savings Bond which is inflation-adjusted. Like ordinary EE Savings Bonds, I Bonds are backed by the United States Treasury. Savings bonds have been around since 1935. Under a temporary name change to War Bonds, US Savings Bonds helped fund World War II. I Bonds have been around since 1998.

I Bonds produce returns with two components. The first is a fixed rate which provides the "real" return - real meaning inflation adjusted. For new purchases, this rate resets semiannually on May 1 and Nov. 1, or the next business day if the first day of the month happens to fall on a holiday. Once you purchase an I Bond, the real rate in force at that time continues for 30 years until the bond matures or until the owner chooses to redeem it. The second component of I Bond return is the inflation rate as represented by the Urban Consumer Price Index (CPI-U), the same index that is used for Social Security resets. The inflation rate used for I Bonds is reset semiannually at the same time the fixed rate is reset, on May 1 and Nov. 1, and the accrued value of your I Bond is updated so that the return compounds semiannually. Note that the fixed rate and the variable rate are reset every six months, the variable rate from the CPI report, the fixed rate at the whim of the Treasury which has sometimes been thought to track the five or ten-year Treasury note.

You can currently buy up to $10,000 annually of electronic I Bonds in a TreasuryDirect account - that's $10,000 per Social Security Number so that you can buy the same amount for your spouse and each of your children. In the past, also you could also request that up to $5000 of your Federal income tax refund be received in the form of a paper I Bond which will be mailed to you whenever the IRS gets around to sending it. That possibility was phased out, however, on January 1, 2025.

Like all Treasury Bonds, I Bonds are not taxable by states and municipalities. Another advantage of I Bonds is that you can choose to defer paying Federal taxes until maturity or redemption. This can be helpful if you or your heir (whom you designate with each purchase) expect to have lower taxable income at some point in the future. Under some circumstances, I Bonds are tax-free when used for educational purposes - read the fine print, though.

One of the most important but seldom noticed qualities of I Bonds is that they're not only an excellent inflation hedge but a hedge against deflation as well. The reason is that the inflation component of the composite return never dips below zero. In the event of a deflationary period, the accrued inflation value of the I Bond would simply freeze at its highest level. When inflation returns, as it always has, the total value of the account begins to compound again from the highest level it has reached.

I Bonds are probably the most flexible investment available. This is because their duration is any date you choose, from one year to their maturity date of 30 years. You cannot redeem your I Bond during the first year, but after that, you choose any moment you wish if for any reason you want to get out. You can thus use them as a place to park cash for the short to intermediate term, or leave them in place and continue to have an assured long-term hedge against inflation.

For the first five years, the price paid for redeeming a bond is giving up one quarter (three months) of returns. After five years of owning an I Bond, the forfeiting of one quarter's returns no longer applies. There are, by the way, no commissions or other costs involved in buying or selling I Bonds. Paper bonds - now available only as part of your tax refund - can be bought at price intervals from $50 to $10,000 while electronic bonds may be bought in penny increments starting at $25. You could, for example, buy an I Bond with a face value $25.23. The Treasury Direct site has a slightly more detailed description which I also recommend that you read. If you have questions I will do my best to respond in comments.

data.title: Rigetti: Washington May Take A Quantum Stake In The Future
data.description:
Since my last Rigetti (NASDAQ:RGTI) analysis, in which I issued a Buy rating, the stock has gained 250% in price. Since that analysis, news has circulated that Washington may be in talks to acquire stakes in quantum companies, including Rigetti. My last analysis leaned bullish, and I am still cautiously optimistic of the return horizon despite a high valuation compared to current intrinsics.

Firstly, it's no surprise that Washington is considering taking a stake in Rigetti, given that it has recently invested in Intel (INTC) (9.9% stake), MP Materials (MP) (DoD 15% stake), Lithium Americas (~5% stake), and U.S. Steel (golden share, control rights). Quantum is important for national security due to the rising nature of quantum decryption and the importance of post-quantum cryptography. A government stake in companies like Rigetti could provide the Department of Defense with more direct access to quantum-hardware development and strengthen America's post-quantum security capabilities.

WSJ & Reuters Reports

The Wall Street Journal says the administration is discussing taking equity stakes in quantum firms like IonQ (IONQ), Rigetti, and D-Wave (QBTS), with a minimum of $10M invested per company. However, it's important to specify that these are not official talks—the Department of Commerce specifically told Reuters it is not currently negotiating with these firms.

That said, Deputy Commerce Secretary Paul Dabbar is reported to be leading the quantum discussions (he previously ran a quantum-networking startup and served as DoE Undersecretary for Science). Dabbar's background raises the follow-through probability somewhat versus typical political/market speculation.

Rigetti's Position

Of the companies mentioned in the reports, my favorite is D-Wave (QBTS) due to recommendation from a trusted source, but for this analysis I have decided to focus on Rigetti.

As of June 30, 2025, the company's current burn allows for approximately 9.6 years of runway when also accounting for liquid investments; a $10M, $25M or even $50M government check would only add around 0.7, 1.7, or 3.4 quarters of runway. Therefore, Rigetti doesn't need the investment, but it provides lower perceived funding risk and cost of capital, likely re-rating the stock and providing sentiment support (which is arguably needed in what is currently deemed a "speculative" sector despite operating in critical future technology). For clarity, cash and equivalents of $57.2M, short-term AFS (liquid investments) of $368.5M, and long-term AFS of $145.8M equals total liquidity of $571.6M. In H1 2025, net cash used in operations was $29.8M, or $14.9M per quarter.

However, a recent Quanta Computing (OTC:QUCCF) collaboration with Rigetti has them each investing at least $250M over five years (R&D and capital), so as spending ramps to meet that commitment, we're looking at a higher forward burn rate and lower runway compared to the H1 comparable.

My takeaway is that this investment is an implicit sovereign "put" if executed, but it doesn't solve error correction or channel/product-market fit for any of the companies in question (the key variables for long-term success). Quantum computing is a risky investment; the companies are pre-earnings and are all early on in development cycles, so I expect if Washington does commit, it will do so with a diversified approach. The risk is that some of the companies may never reach profitability if technology or commercialization stalls, and Washington's stakes wouldn't change this.

Investment Case

Quantum stocks have recently contracted from all-time highs, and I'm leaning cautiously bullish.

Rigetti has a forward price-to-sales ratio of ~1,500. This is admittedly high on the surface. Yet, one needs to put it into perspective with the likelihood of ~100% annual revenue growth rates over multiple years ahead with successful commercial traction. This is very early on in Rigetti's lifecycle, so an "extreme" valuation can be tolerated durably by the market; even if much of the company's future growth is already priced in, it's a company that will likely command a premium valuation even when at scale. Rigetti should only be invested in if you understand that heavy volatility is almost certain, and entry points should be considered carefully and not rushed based on herd hysteria.

Even so, in a conservative bull case, based on my technical analysis, the stock is likely to reach $60 or higher in 12 months, which indicates a 50% return. I base this on momentum factors and recent RSI patterns, exposed in the chart above. However, that requires a significant reduction in sentiment based on moderating growth. If the ultra-premium sustains with a forward P/S ratio of 1,000 in December 2026, December 2027 revenue of $40M, and shares outstanding of 375 million, the stock will be worth over $100 per share in slightly over 12 months, a 150% increase from present. This outcome is not unreasonable, but the return horizon leans speculative because buying any investment pre-earnings and before commercial validation can lead to stark volatility as the competitive landscape evolves. Put simply, diversification or heavy conviction in a single quantum company is the pre-requisite to attaining reliable alpha investing in the industry.

Investment Risks

In reality, the recent price increases in these quantum stocks, including Rigetti, have been based on talks, and officials have even stated that they are "not in talks." So these recent sentiment surges are based on 'no talks,' or rumors. Hopefully that outlines the speculative nature of this horizon and shows why you should be careful in your allocation framework.

Also, even passive government stakes dilute and can complicate foreign sales/licensing and eligibility for future grants, which was a risk outlined in the Intel deal explicitly. As any quantum deals would likely use Intel as a pseudo-template, the market can learn of the double-edged sword related to the allocation before it's potentially official. Market presence could be both supported to the downside and, ironically, internationally capped to the upside (for example, in sensitive geopolitical regions like China, these quantum companies would probably not operate).

Moreover, fault-tolerant, utility-scale systems remain years away. Annealing versus gate-model divergence is shaping near-term TAM and margins. Rigetti operates gate-model machines, so has longer-term potential compared to annealing's near-term market presence, but this market is still subject to disruption, company-specific setbacks, and consolidation by one or two primary leaders.

Conclusion: Buy

In conclusion, we're really looking at a heavy optionality investment. Rigetti is certainly not a core holding and deserves a small moonshot weight in portfolios comfortable with risk. Investing at the present price is by no means a value investment; in fact, the price is only reasonable on the daily-intervals chart, which suggests caution is warranted and downside should be tolerated amid a medium-term horizon even if net gains are alpha producing.

In my opinion, it's often best not to "bet" in the markets. If you do make moonshots bets, there's a typically reliable way to do this for those who win long-term. Markets are analyzed for years, and specific companies are courted and scrutinized. Stakes and whole organizations are not acquired because of rumors; to the contrary, patience is deployed so that each allocation has a meaningful chance of multi-decade upside via the likelihood of management conquering their target markets. Any investment worth its salt will rise in six months as well as today, unless it's a cyclical trade (which quantum is not).

Those with a penchant for risk may take the Druckenmiller path, "invest, then research," for some investments require pace to achieve great value. But then, it's important to never be afraid to sell if the thesis changes.

Maybe a small tester position now, and then an increase once results are proven and Washington is potentially already involved. Building a position with staggered confidence beats big "bets" that fail under pressure due to unpredictable outcomes, especially when they are first made based on rumors and not fundamental strengths.

data.title: FedEx: Tune Out The Noise And Buy This Bargain
data.description:
The market continues to favor expensive stocks, with the S&P 500 (SPY) trading at a forward PE of 25.5. It’s important to keep in mind, however, that this isn’t representative of most stocks. That’s because the market is heavily weighted by a handful of companies with trillion-dollar market caps. This means that there are plenty of bargains to be had so long as one has a time horizon longer than 1 year, and ideally 5-10 years.

This brings me to FedEx (NYSE:FDX), which currently trades at around half the valuation of SPY with a forward PE of just 13.1. I last covered FDX back in July, highlighting its cost savings initiatives that are expected to drive margin expansion and attractive valuation despite near-term headwinds. FDX remains attractively valued. At its current price of $238, FDX sits solidly in the bottom half of its 52-week range, as shown below.

In this article, I revisit FDX including recent business results, and discuss why its currently a bargain in this frothy market for value, income and growth, so let’s get started!

Why FDX?

FedEx is a global logistics leader, operating FedEx Express, FedEx Round, and FedEx Freight. Its worldwide infrastructure is matched only by peer UPS (UPS). Over the past 12 months, FDX generated $89 billion in total revenue. It serves over 200 million customers and operates in over 220 countries.

Its no secret that FDX has seen headwinds from softening global trade, particularly in the China-to-U.S. route. This is was pressured by the removal of the U.S. de minimis exemption for low-value imports, meaning that even low priced goods from online marketplaces like Shein have import taxes.

Despite external pressures such as the one noted above, FDX still managed to grow consolidated revenue by 3% YoY during fiscal Q1 2026 (ended August 31, 2025). Also, average daily volume grew by 5% YoY. This was driven by strength in U.S. domestic package services.

In particular, FedEx Express’ revenue and adjusted operating income rose by 4% and 17%, respectively. Operating margin in this segment rose by 70 basis points, marking its fourth consecutive quarter of higher profitability. As shown below, the Express segment’s growth more than offset weakness in the Freight business during fiscal Q1.

Moreover, adjusted operating income rose by a robust 7% and adjusted EPS grew by 6% YoY to $3.83. This was due to operating margin expansion of 20 basis points, as FDX benefited from ongoing cost and productivity programs across its network. FDX made strong headway on its cost transformation initiatives, with it achieving $200 million in cost savings during fiscal Q1 alone. FDX’s faster operating income growth (compared to revenue growth) was also due to it diversifying into high-margin verticals like healthcare.

Management is guiding for full fiscal year revenue growth of 5% and $1 billion in cost savings from ongoing transformation initiatives. The cost savings are expected to be partially offset by an estimated $1 billion operating income headwind from trade-related pressures. This includes weaker China export demand and increased customs clearance costs, among others.

Notwithstanding trade-related headwinds, FDX is positioning itself for long-term profitability and growth. This is reflected by solid progress in its Network 2.0 initiative, which aims to create a fully integrated U.S. ground network. Thus far, 360 U.S. and Canadian stations have been optimized with 3 million average daily packages now flowing through the new network model.

Management is also shifting resources by reducing Asia outbound traffic by 25% this year while allocating resources to more profitable Asia-Europe lanes. Plus, the planned spin-off of the Freight business in June 2026 could unlock value for shareholders. That’s because this could smooth out the revenue stream as the Freight business is more cyclical and tied to the industrial economy. This would also give investors direct exposure to a leading pure-play LTL (less than truckload) company while making the remaining business easier to value.

FDX maintains a solid balance sheet with a BBB credit rating from S&P. This is supported by $6.2 billion in cash on hand and a safe net debt to TTM EBITDA ratio of 2.84x. This supports the 2.4% dividend yield, which is well-covered by a 31% payout ratio.

While the dividend yield isn’t particularly high, it does come with a robust 16.8% 5-year CAGR. FDX also returns ample cash to shareholders through stock buybacks. This includes $500 million in share repurchases during fiscal Q1 alone. As shown below, FDX has removed 11% of its outstanding float over the past 5 years.

Lastly, I see solid value in FDX at the current price of $238 with a forward PE of 13.1, sitting well below its historical PE of 16, as shown below.

At the current valuation, the market appears to be overly rotated on tariff impacts including the removal of the de minimis exemption. At the same time, it seems to be ignoring long-term catalysts from Network 2.0 cost optimization, business simplicity from the upcoming spin-of of the Freight business, and reallocation of resources toward higher margin businesses.

While EPS growth is guided to be flat this fiscal year, analysts estimate 11% to 16% annual EPS growth starting next fiscal year through fiscal 2029. I believe these are reasonable estimates for the aforementioned reasons. This, combined with the dividend yield, share buybacks and a potential return to the mean valuation could result in robust annual total returns in the 15% to 20% range.

Risks to FDX include integration risks as it relates to its merging of Express and Ground networks into one single platform (like that of peer UPS). This could come with significant upfront costs and execution delays due to complexity. FDX remains reliant on mega-shippers like Amazon (AMZN), Walmart (WMT), and Target (TGT), which could increasingly rely on their own networks. Plus, FDX workers could unionize, like that of UPS, which could drive higher labor costs.

Investor Takeaway

FedEx stands out as a compelling value play in today’s expensive market, trading at a forward PE at roughly half that of the S&P 500. This is despite solid execution on cost savings, margin expansion, and long-term strategic initiatives like Network 2.0.

Plus, FDX is actively reallocating resources to higher-margin verticals and preparing to unlock shareholder value through the upcoming spin-off of its Freight segment. Lastly, FDX carries a strong balance sheet, has consistent share buybacks and a well-covered dividend. FDX is positioned to deliver potentially strong double-digit total returns from its current discounted valuation. As such, savvy investors could do well by looking past the short-term noise while focusing on the long-term runway.

data.title: Visa Q4 Earnings Preview: Low Volatility Expected
data.description:
A company you can rely on

Visa (NYSE:V) is one of my biggest positions in my portfolio and it is about to release Q4 FY2025 results on 28th October. As stated in my previous articles, I consider Visa as one of the best companies in world as it presents a huge competitive advantage in a growing secular trend, namely that of digital payments.

The more people spend, the more Visa earns through its fees. In a consumer-driven society and more inclined to avoid cash payments, Visa’s business model is sustained by two significant growth catalysts. Furthermore, the dominance in this industry makes it possible to achieve a profit margin above 50%, something that not even the best tech company can match.

Besides the rock-solid fundamentals, the company is used to repurchase its own shares, further fueling the EPS growth. It is really hard to find a flaw in this company, but probably there is one: its valuation.

Price to Free Cash Flow is in line with historical average, so I don’t believe the company is currently undervalued; this is what is preventing me to buy more shares.

However, on 28th October the company will release its Q4 FY2025 results: could this be a good time to buy some shares?

What analysts expect from Q4 FY2025

Analysts are confident that Q4 FY2025 will be a positive quarter overall, even though less rosy than Q3 FY2025.

  • EPS are expected to be $2.96, a 9.20% increase compared to Q4 FY2024.
  • Revenues should reach $10.61 billion, a 10.30% improvement compared to Q4 FY2024.

As you can see, nothing so surprising. As usual, Visa should grow low-double digits overall. Let’s now see the forecasts of each business segment.

  • Service revenue: this is the business where Visa charges to the financial institutions the service fee. For those unaware, it’s the fee that banks pay when issue a Visa card and exploit its payment ecosystem. This revenue is calculated on the payment volume, namely the total dollar amount that Visa cards process. The latter is expected to reach $233.17 billion in Q4 FY2025, an increase of 15.43% compared to last year. The revenue that is expected to be generated out of payment volume is $4.63 billion, a 10.30% increase year-over-year.
  • Data processing: this business is what Visa charges to process transactions. You know those few seconds you wait for your card to be accepted for payment? In that time lapse, Visa checks if you have enough money to proceed and transfers the fund between the two banks involved in the transaction. Unlike the previous one, this segment is based on number of transactions and it is the most important one in terms of revenue. Analysts anticipate revenue of $5.23 billion for the quarter to come., an increase of 13.50% year-over-year.
  • International transaction: this business is what Visa charges for cross-border transaction, basically when the two banks involved in the transaction don’t belong to the same country. This business is separate from the others as it tends to be more complex due to exchange rates and different policies between the two countries. As a results, Visa charges higher fees for these transactions. Analysts expect $3.86 billion from this business in Q4 FY2025, a 11.30% increase year-over-year.
  • Other: this business includes multiple activities, from consulting and fraud management, to digital services and data analytics. We can consider it as a sort of mix, disconnected from the typical business core. It is the least important segment in terms of revenue, but the one that is growing the most. Analysts expect $1.18 billion revenue in Q4 FY2025, a 22.30% year-over-year.

Overall, all the segments should grow low-double digits, expect Other that will probably expand twice as much.

What I expect from Q4 FY2025

My goal is to increase my position in Visa, but I am looking for the right moment to do so. I am basically waiting for a drop to $300-$310 per share, but I don’t think the next quarterly report release will create this opportunity. Actually, I predict a rather low volatility on that day.

As you can see from previous earnings release date, the market reaction was typically flat most of times. The last volatile quarterly release was on 23rd July 2024 following a slight revenue miss, but it was a fairly unusual event. To observe a volatile period, we should get back to late-2021/2022, a period full of uncertainties because of the wars, high inflation and raising interest rates.

Nowadays, I don’t see how Visa could drop/rise significantly following the Q4 FY2025 release. The stock market is heading toward all-time high and real GDP is improving pretty fast, driven by consumer spending. Consequently, higher payment volume/transaction fees for Visa. But the market already knows it, that is why in the current valuation is already priced in a low-double digits growth.

Visa’s business model is designed to be procyclical, so as long as the economy stays healthy, it is very unlikely that we can witness a negative quarterly report. In light of this, I think the market reaction will be in line with the recent ones, so within a range of -2%/+2%. So, unfortunately, I don’t see the correction coming, but I hope to be wrong.

data.title: EQT: Profits Return Amid Lower Costs
data.description:
EQT (NYSE:EQT) completed the Equitrans acquisition in the third quarter of the last fiscal year. That acquisition promised to lower some costs materially. Even though natural gas prices were higher in the current quarter, the profit comparison to the loss registered in the third quarter comparison is stark. Clearly, the lower costs envisioned from the acquisition benefited the company in the current quarter.

A side issue here is that just about every acquisition came with some midstream assets. Those assets allowed for a faster transition to get more natural gas out of the basin. Probably the largest effect by far was the startup of the Mountain Valley Pipeline that came with the Equitrans acquisition.

In this fashion, management has done more to improve the price received for natural gas faster than one would have thought. There is still likely more work to be done in this area. But just getting the discount down by reducing the relative amount of sales of natural gas into an oversupplied market is an accomplishment.

Income Statement

There is a sizable amount of "other operating costs" affecting the income statement comparison.

Note that both Transportation and Processing costs as well as Other Operating Costs are lower in the year-to-date comparison than was the case in the previous fiscal year. Those reduced costs boosted the return to income.

The issue with "other operating costs" is that even though each set of those costs is likely considered nonrecurring, there is a decent chance of similar costs existing in future earnings comparisons from each acquisition made.

This company has long grown through acquisitions as well as organic growth. Therefore, opportunistic acquisitions are likely to play a material part of the growth story in the future for quite a while.

It should be noted that the Rice Brothers sold the last company they built. Whether this one will be sold for the right price is an open question.

Acquiring Equitrans was supposed to reduce Gathering costs significantly. That appears to be precisely what has happened. What happened was a wider profit margin at the same commodity price because of the lower costs. That played a huge part in the dramatic return to a lot of profitability with only a slight increase in natural gas prices.

Q3 Highlights

Probably the most significant effect of all the moves talked about before is the dramatic increase in free cash flow shown below:

The company already has earnings that are near the top of the better years from the past. But this time around, free cash flow has made a giant leap in terms of progress.

The reason for all that free cash flow is likely the repeated acquisitions of midstream capacity because pipelines often generate a lot of cash flow with minimal capital needs. Therefore, as each midstream interest was acquired, the free cash flow likely took a step forward. The biggest step came with the acquisition of Equitrans Midstream because it was a fairly large midstream interest compared to many that were made.

But make no mistake that this is a rare natural gas producer with a significant vertical integration into the midstream business. That midstream business is likely to make earnings a bit less volatile than is the case for many upstream competitors while providing lower costs compared to many competitors. Overall, this vertical integration appears to be a very good deal for shareholders.

As the company optimizes operations of these acquisitions, there could be some more free cash flow growth in the future.

Remaining Key Points

Management signed some LNG agreements that will not begin for a few years. The reason for this is that management believes that initially LNG will be oversupplied for a bit and that better pricing will exist some years from now that would make the LNG business worthwhile.

Also note that net debt is crossing the $8 billion mark on its way to a targeted $5 billion.

The deleveraging process demonstrates how investment-grade companies handle deleveraging compared to some companies I follow that struggle for years with debt issues. An investment-grade company whips the debt situation into shape relatively quickly. This is especially true compared to some debt-heavy, below-investment-grade companies I follow.

Also note that the company raised its quarterly dividend yet again.

One of the things that may happen is that, due to the relatively large size of the company, each acquisition may represent a smaller percentage of the business. This could imply relatively fast assimilations in the future, as was the case with the latest acquisition. It could also mean that each acquisition will have steadily fewer effects on future relevant quarterly earnings.

Summary

The reported earnings are benefiting from the original guidance that the acquisition of Equitrans would materially lower the company breakeven point. This was combined with stronger natural gas prices to produce a big jump in free cash flow.

There could be additional growth ahead from add-on projects that typically pay back fairly quickly and other optimization measures.

It is very likely that opportunistic acquisitions will be a significant part of the future growth story, given the significance of acquisitions already made since this management took over. This is a company that is likely to return annual double-digit appreciation and total return well into the future.

The company is well positioned to benefit from the anticipated strengthening of natural gas prices.

Additionally, during the conference call, management did mention some future liquids production even though the company is expected to remain a largely dry gas proposition.

Though the company has improved dramatically from when this management took over after a proxy battle (before covid), there is still more potential profitability improvement ahead.

This company, therefore, remains an investment-grade "Strong Buy" idea. I would likely hold it as long as the current management is in charge, as the company under this management has accrued a tremendous growth record that one seldom sees in this industry.

Risks

Any company that grows by acquisition could make an acquisition that fails to meet expectations. That risk does decline as management gains experience making acquisitions to acquire a history of successful acquisitions.

The Rice Brothers gained control of EQT in 2019. Since then, they have transformed the company into a financial juggernaut. A loss of their personnel services could prove critical to the company's future.

The anticipated improvement in natural gas prices could be delayed or not occur at all.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Why I'm Watching This Subtle Market Shift
data.description:
By Mike Larson

It’s subtle. But it’s there. I’m talking about the modest rotation out of more offensive sectors and in to more defensive ones.

Look at these two MoneyShow Charts of the Day. The first shows the relative performance of the Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLV) versus the Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLF). The second shows the same ratio comparison between the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLP) and the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

Chart 1: Health Care Vs. Financials

(Source: StockCharts.com)

Chart 2: Consumer Staples Vs. Consumer Discretionary

(Source: StockCharts.com)

What stands out? Health care stocks are “breaking out” relative to financials after a long period of underperformance. You’re seeing the same thing for staples stocks versus discretionary ones, although in that case it’s a more muted move.

While I didn’t include a chart here, I’d also note that the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) has returned roughly 7.5% in the last three months. Bond prices move in the opposite direction of bond yields, so that’s being accompanied by a sharp drop in the latter. The yield on the benchmark 10-year Treasury Note just sank below 4% briefly, for instance. That was its lowest level since April.

Add it all up and I’d say markets are increasingly betting on economic weakness. Again, it’s subtle. It’s early. But it’s worth watching. Because if this trend were to continue, I’d probably dial back my “Be Bold” stance on markets.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Wall Street Roundup: Are We In A Bubble?
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Tesla headlines earnings this week (0:25). GM pops, Netflix slides (1:50). Intel's strong results (5:50). Beyond Meat's meme stock moment (7:00). CPI report, rate cuts (8:35). Interesting AI earnings coming next week (10:25).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, our Director of news at Seeking Alpha, always great to talk to you on these Wall Street Roundups. Welcome back to another week.

Brian Stewart: Great to be here.

RS: We've got a government shutdown that still exists. We've got some tariff news with Canada. We've got major earnings. Where should we start this week?

BS: It's funny. I kinda forgot about the government shutdown. It's so pushed to the background.

I think this week has all been about earnings getting revved up. Tesla (TSLA) obviously, the headliner just in terms of the profile the company has, though the earnings themselves were neither here nor there.

The stock was up about 2% after earnings. It missed on its bottom line, and its margin shrank, but revenue was up 12% and beat expectations.

The company warned that it's facing near term uncertainty with shifting trade, tariff and fiscal policy, though I think we all knew that.

So that wasn't really new information, though slightly noteworthy that the company felt the need to to acknowledge that.

I think in general, people took the Tesla report as fine, a step on the way of the comeback that started after Musk returned to full time duties after the DOGE experiment.

So I think investors are basically in a wait and see mode. The stock has already recovered quite a bit from what it lost during the DOGE situation.

And so I think really they're just looking to the future and and I think deliveries are gonna matter and production's gonna matter as those reports come out in the coming months.

RS: And there's many more companies to get into when it comes to earnings. What else would you put on that list?

BS: As long as we're talking EVs, (GM) is an interesting one.

It popped 15% after its earnings. It beat expectations, raised guidance. The fundamental lesson from that was that the tariff impact was not as bad as the company expected. Meanwhile, GM has been increasing its market share.

It reached its highest market share since 2017. So the company's executing well. There's worries of an EV demand softening as people went out and bought EVs as the tax credit was expiring.

And so there's there's a worry, and this is something that could impact Tesla as well. There's a worry that this sort of packed the channel in that regard. But overall, investors were very upbeat about GM's report. And it just goes to show that the car industry is more than just Tesla.

And then on the downside, the big loser, at least among the headline stocks, was Netflix (NFLX).

It dropped 10% after its earnings. Revenue rose 17% from last year, beating expectations where the company took a 600,000,000 charge related to an ongoing dispute with Brazilian tax authorities. It also saw some margin decline.

I think that margin decline is probably more of a worry than the Brazilian tax situation which will resolve itself eventually. It's kind of a near term hurdle, but not really an existential threat to the company.

I think investors are probably a little bit more worried about margins shrinking a little bit.

RS: We had Hardika Singh on from Fundstrat on Investing Experts this past week on Sunday. And she was talking about how there's such an opportunity to be buying the dip these days as the stock market moves in, let's say, unprecedented ways and things that are sometimes hard to understand, but the opportunities are there.

And as you mentioned Netflix, Cathie Wood, she added, she bought the dip on Netflix. Anything to say there or what you're seeing from market participants or anything to note about Cathie's position or any other large positions that are being taken there or anything else to note with Netflix?

BS: I think you're getting at the fundamental debate that's going on in the market right now. I think a lot of people are worried that the market as a whole and the AI stocks specifically are gonna turn into a pumpkin sometime soon.

It's gonna strike midnight, and we're all gonna find ourselves running without shoes on, if that metaphor stretches that far. I mean, Cathie Wood is a super bull, always has been.

So I don't know, she's looking long term, right? I mean, her idea is that these stocks are going to sort of change the fundamental way in which we do business and live our lives.

And so I don't know how much she's factoring in near term factors like tariffs and things like that. So I think that goes to the heart of the debate, to what your horizon is.

I mean, if you have a 10 horizon, certainly there's opportunities to get stocks pretty cheaply. Now, if you have a much more near term, if you're more of a trader, there are a lot of worries out there.

And so that's the I think the question that's being asked is when a stock like Netflix drops 10%, is that the start of a bubble bursting or is that the best opportunity you're going to get for the next foreseeable future to buy that stock?

RS: And to the point of long term investing in that space, Dan Rayburn, an expert in the media and streaming space, he writes and speaks a lot outside of Seeking Alpha. We've had him on a few times on Investing Experts. And the last episode he was on, we called it Netflix is Still King. He espouses a bullish long term perspective on that.

We saw results from Intel (INTC) on Thursday. What would you say about that? Tech obviously top of mind for many investors as it has such a large effect on the rest of the market.

BS: Last check today, the company reported last night, stock is up about 2%.

It had strong results and guidance. People see this as signs that the turnaround at Intel is proceeding a pace. There are still worries about valuation and worries about execution.

The stock has rallied significantly recently. It's up 57% since NVIDIA (NVDA) announced its investment in mid September. So it's had a huge upswing over the past few weeks, a very short period of time.

And so now I think it's a matter of investors in a show me kind of mood. So we'll see in the coming quarters whether Intel can turn what seems to be a switch in momentum for the company into real bottom line results.

RS: Any other stock specific highlights that you would share with listeners?

BS: Just to straying off of earnings a little bit, I think it's worth it as we talk about the debate around whether or not we're in a bubble.

Beyond Meat (BYND) had a a meme stock moment this past week. So it jumped from 52¢ at the close of last Thursday to $3.62 on Tuesday. It hit $7.69 along the way.

There was some news to kind of sprinkle in there and drive the stock. It had an expanded deal with Walmart (WMT), but this was mostly a short squeeze meme stock situation, and there were sizable gains.

I mean, nothing in the the hundreds of percentage points that you saw from Beyond Meat, but Krispy Kreme (DNUT), GoPro (GPRO), iRobot (IRBT), also substantial. And these were all sort of the cluster of meme stocks that have emerged in 2025.

So I think if you're skeptical about the market in general, I think situations like this raise a red flag when you see frothy situations like that.

If your argument is that we're in a bubble, then these kind of situations pop up and it shows that there's there's kind of a a lack of rationality in the market.

I think a bull would just say we've had meme stocks going on through the twenty twenties. It's just part of the market at this point. Every once in a while, this stuff is gonna bubble up, and you can't really make longer term decisions based on how often this is happening.

RS: Hardika had another point in that episode about the meme stock ETF (MEME) that it just recently came back on board. So I think reflective of that point for sure.

I mentioned the government shutdown at the beginning. You said you weren't very focused on it, but we have had a dearth of data as we've talked about because of that shutdown.

We did get the CPI report this week. Some bullishness if you wanna dive into that pool. What would you say about that report or economic data in general these days?

BS: That's a good point about the economic data, especially we're theoretically two weeks away from getting the next jobs report.

Pretty skeptical that's gonna happen. I think there were headlines today that the next CPI report is in danger as well. So this might be the last CPI that we get for some period of time.

But pretty much in line with what people were expecting, the inflation rate, the core CPI was 3%, which is where we sort of settled.

Officially, the Fed is still targeting a 2% inflation rate, but I think there's sort of an understanding that 3% is fine. You know, like, we wish we had 2%, but we'll settle for 3%.

So the biggest thing in the economic calendar next week is the Fed decision. Everyone pretty much expects the Fed to cut rates by 25 basis points. That's pretty much baked in 97% chance if you look at the trading so far.

Also, another rate cut is expected for December. It's roughly fifty fifty if there's gonna be a third rate cut in January. So people are expecting the Fed to take these stair steps lower in the next several meetings.

And so as long as inflation doesn't spike anywhere beyond sort of that 3% range, I think that rate cutting situation is going to unfold as the market expects.

RS: We've got some major earnings coming next week to continue with the crux of earning season. What would you highlight for investors?

BS: I think an interesting dichotomy to look at is Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Meta (META), both AI plays, both coming out next week.

So its last earnings reports, it spiked 11% in the day after the earnings, but has since drifted off that level. So it's down about 5% from that post earnings jump.

Basically, there's a valuation worry at Meta. There's also a spending concern. It's sort of a habit at Meta to push a lot of chips in the middle when it sees an idea that happened with the metaverse and now it's happening with AI.

And so the question is whether these bets are going to to pay off. I mean, the core business, the Facebook business, the social media business has been extremely strong.

And the question is, did the cash that it's investing from these businesses into the new businesses, are those eventually going to fall to the bottom line?

And so that's gonna be what people have their eye on in Meta. So there was like a a rush of enthusiasm after the last earnings report, which was extremely strong, and then a caution that set in afterwards.

Meanwhile, Google, after it released its last earnings report, which is also extremely strong, it largely received a shrug.

The stock didn't move much at all after that earnings report. However, the stock has been making its way higher ever since. So it's up about 36% since that last earnings report.

So the opposite reaction to Meta where there was sort of a indifference to the the actual earnings report, but then kind of a drift up as enthusiasm grew over the past several months.

So it'll be interesting to see when Google's report comes out, when Alphabet's report comes out, whether or not the results can justify the enthusiasm that's now been priced in.

RS: Anything else to add earnings wise or next week wise?

BS: We've got Apple (AAPL) coming out. The new iPhone came out, so there there might be some commentary on that.

Though I think probably the next quarter will be the key one for that. Another interesting thing about Apple is Apple is up only about 5% this year and only turned positive for the year in September.

It's another stock that slipped a little after its last earnings report in July and it's up about 30% since then. So like Alphabet, it's sort of seen a growing enthusiasm over the past several months.

In Apple's case, part of the upside that it has seen is that it made a $100,000,000,000 manufacturing commitment to the US, which was seen as a hedge against the tariff situation.

So Apple's pretty exposed to the trade tensions between China and the US, So that $100,000,000,000 commitment is seen as a way to get an exemption if in fact those tariffs really bite down on the company.

It saw a boost in the August, September time frame as that worry, the tariff worry, seemed to get relieved.

And then Microsoft (MSFT) is also reporting next week, a company that also is an AI play. Look at the Azure numbers as that come out, the cloud numbers.

It saw 26% growth in its cloud business last quarter, which was up from 21% in the previous quarter and 20% the year before. So it stepped up to a higher growth plane with that last quarter. I think the question is whether it's going to continue to see that higher growth rate or whether it's gonna retreat back to where it was before.

data.title: Pagaya: The Foundation Is Set For A Long-Term Rerating
data.description:
Introduction

I earlier covered technology-driven Chinese lending businesses like Finvolution (FINV) and QFIN Holdings (QFIN). What stood out for these businesses was their business model, which uses AI for inclusion of borrowers that were rejected by the traditional banking system. One such business operating in the US is Pagaya (NASDAQ:PGY). While not exactly the same business model, PGY too uses technology to serve the borrowers that are rejected by the traditional banking system.

While this segment is not served by the traditional banking system due to its high-risk nature, PGY is trying to solve this problem with the help of advanced AI technology to screen creditworthy borrowers among this underserved category. This leaves PGY with a large unserved market and Total Addressable Market (TAM).

Recently the stock has started to turn profitable after many years of investment and building trust among its partners. Now it seems like PGY is going through a pivotal moment where operational leverage would kick in, leading to fast bottom-line growth. Additionally, PGY's valuation also seems to be decent to me, especially considering the ramp-up in profitability going forward. Therefore, I am issuing a buy rating on the stock.

Company overview

You can compare PGY to a scrap collector in the lending industry. When a loan application is rejected by one of the lending partners of PGY it is then sent to PGY for further screening. PGY screens these loan applications through its proprietary AI and machine learning technology and pulls out potential creditworthy loan applicants that might have been rejected by the traditional screening process of lending partners. Once screened, PGY sends a loan proposal to the partner lender, and then the lender sends it to the borrowers. Once the loan proposal is accepted, it creates a temporary asset facilitated by PGY, also known as network volume.

PGY keeps its balance sheet light by funding these loans either through a financing vehicle or securitizing these loans into asset-backed securities (ABS) or through a pre-funded model where it raises capital from institutional investors before assets are created. This process makes PGY a pure-play technology company that facilitates asset creation (providing value to borrowers and lenders) and securitization (providing value to investors) and thus generates a commission for itself, which it terms as "take rate."

A Turn to Profitability

After many quarters of being in GAAP losses, PGY has finally turned into GAAP profits starting from the first quarter of 2025.

The company has been posting consistent growth in network volume growth over the last many quarters. However, there is a subtle shift in the last few quarters, with the company now shifting toward other segments as they have solidified their position in the personal loan category. The share of auto and POS loans has been rising over the last few quarters. The share has risen from 9% a year ago to ~30% now. Furthermore, management plans to take this share to 1/3rd each for all three loan categories, cumulatively generating $25 billion in annual network volume from the current run rate of $10.5-11.5 billion. This looks to be quite a good strategy, as the company could leverage its existing relationship with partners to penetrate into other loan categories to pursue growth.

With respect to profitability, PGY measures its operational efficiency through a non-GAAP financial metric that they call Fee Revenue Less Production Costs (FRLPC). FLPC is calculated by subtracting production costs from fee revenue, which is compensation to lending partners for acquiring and originating assets. The company reported a near-record-high FRLPC margin (~4.8%) in the most recent quarter, driven by improved monetization in the personnel loan category, which is starting to mature, leading to a better take rate. The improvement on the FRLPC front is having a leveraging effect on the bottom line of the company, leading to the turn into profitability.

Outlook

I believe PGY's recent turnaround has legs, and it should extend in years to come as the company would now reap the benefits of years of brand building and investments. The company has 31 lending partners with a 100% retention rate, including names like US Bank, SoFi, Ally, and Klarna. On the investment side the company has an ABS investor base of ~145 entities.

As PGY has established itself as a trusted partner for both investors and lending partners, I believe it would be easier now to partner with more and bigger partners. As per management, they are targeting to add at least 2-4 lending partners per year with ongoing discussion with 80% of the top 25 banks in the US.

Having multiple partners is important, as it will give PGY access to a larger pool of potential borrowers to source from. This not only will increase PGY's network volume but also increase the data points for PGY, which it could later use to further train its AI and machine learning models.

As per PGY's management, they see a combined $850 billion worth of serviceable addressable market within the personal, auto, and POS loan categories. This in itself is quite a huge market; furthermore, the company can also leverage its relationship with existing partners to further expand into other loan categories, just like it is doing in the POS and auto segments. And frankly, they had done quite a commendable job in the POS and auto expansion by growing the share from 9% to 30% within a year. This shows how PGY can leverage its existing relation and its own goodwill to expand into other loan categories. So for the network volume, I believe PGY has a huge headroom to grow for at least the next 5 to 10 years given the huge end market.

In addition to good network volume growth and subsequently topline growth, I believe PGY's bottom line growth would not only grow but grow at a much faster pace thanks to the operating leverage. As mentioned earlier, PGY has earlier invested quite a sum in itself to build all the necessary infrastructure, partnerships, and goodwill in order to grow.

Now, as everything now seems to be in place, PGY would need increasingly less expenditure to grow in its huge TAM. If we look at recent quarters, core Opex as a percentage of FRLPC has consistently trended downward, signaling operational leverage at play. I believe this leverage should continue to benefit the company in the future as well, leading to faster bottom-line growth.

What to expect from 3Q25

PGY is set to announce its 3Q25 results on the 10th of November. I believe current momentum should persist in the upcoming quarter report, and it would be obvious to expect another quarter with GAAP profits.

I would be keeping an eye on the network volume growth, as to how fast it grows; as per management, they are expecting a network volume of $2.75 billion to $2.95 billion. Any kind of positive surprise on this metric would be taken very positively by the street.

Another important metric that I would be looking for would be the FLRPC margin. While this margin is near the upper end of management's long-term guidance range. I believe the possibility of this margin overshooting the long-term guidance of 4-5% is quite high, as the recent ramp-up in auto and POS becomes more profitable in the coming quarter after a less profitable ramp-up period. So I would be expecting a surprise on this front either in the upcoming quarter or in quarters after that.

Lastly, while the delinquency ratio has remained stable for the last many quarters, I would be keeping an eye on this given the recent asset quality concerns across the banking industry that surfaced during the ongoing results season.

Risk

PGY's business model revolves around sourcing creditworthy borrowers from a pool of potential borrowers. Sourcing creditworthy borrowers is a key value proposition of PGY, so if there are situations where PGY's sourced loans become bad more frequently, i.e., the delinquency rate rises, then this could be a problem for the entire business model of PGY.

Partners that provide investments to buy out these packaged loans sourced by PGY might lose trust in PGY's ability to source high-quality loans, leading to lower demand for its product.

Fortunately, PGY's credit quality is quite robust, and delinquency ratios in personal and auto loans are either stable or improving. Moreover, the investing partner base is consistently growing.

Is PGY worth buying now

I prefer to value a lending business based on its price-to-book ratio in conjunction with ROE. The price-to-book ratio tells how much premium the market is willing to give to a business, and ROE tells how efficiently a business is utilizing its book value to generate return.

For PGY, its current forward price-to-book ratio is trending near 4.48x, which indicates that the market is assigning a hefty premium for PGY's business. Meanwhile, if we look at the ROE, it currently is negative given PGY's losses in the earlier quarter; however, since PGY is turning around, it should report a significant surge in profits in the next few quarters. If we use the consensus EPS estimate of $2.77 for 2025, we would arrive at 38% forward ROE (ROE calculated by EPS/book value per share).

Based on the 38% ROE and 4.48x P/B ratio, one could arrive at a yield ratio (ROE/price to book) (a measure to value a company based on market premium and capital efficiency) of 8.5%.

If we compare the yield ratio of PGY with its peers I belive PGY is decently placed. In PGY's case, I believe its business model deserves a premium, given that it is developing a moat-like structure as a trusted screener and consolidator of loans. Furthermore, I believe PGY's ROE has good upside potential given it has just started to turn around, and operating leverage would make it produce more return on lesser incremental capital, leading to a potential expansion in ROE and yield ratio going forward.

Company Ticker P/B (TTM) BVPS EPS Est. 2025 2025 ROE Yield ratio
Pagaya Technologies PGY 4.57 7.13 2.77 38.88% 8.51%
LendingTree TREE 6.06 8.89 4.31 48.48% 8.00%
LendingClub LC 1.41 13.02 1.14 8.76% 6.21%
Chime Financial CHYM 2.96 3.89 -4.24 -109.08% -36.85%
OneMain Holdings OMF 1.96 28.84 6.36 22.05% 11.25%

Given decent valuations and a potential of ROE expansion going forward, I believe PGY is a good buy.

data.title: 2008 Vs. 2025: 3 Similarities, And 3 Ways To Avoid The Same Fate
data.description:
Once again, I'm here not to tell anyone what to do with their money. My job here is to share my experience, including decades of research into a wide range of investment strategies and vehicles. Prior to the emergence of ETFs, I analyzed mutual funds for my then-advisory clients, did a little stock investing, and on 3 separate occasions managed a mutual fund.

The first one started on August 14, 2008. What happened next? This:

That's a 46% decline in under 7 months. Not because of anything that investors did not know about in 2007, or even as far back at 2006. Housing market woes, debt build up, overconfidence on the part of investors, etc.

The fund I managed was of the "alternative" variety. I used mutual funds and ETFs, and allocated to them in an approach that was essentially a liquid version of a hedge fund of funds. I used the very first covered call mutual funds and closed-end funds back then. I've written briefly about those here before, those predecessors of the popular and ubiquitous ones of today, a market now dominated by names like JP Morgan Asset Management, NEOS and Yield Max.

Ads like this are seemingly everywhere now. They offer big time income rates.

Other features? Who cares! Well, I care. This article is not mainly about covered call ETFs. I've said my piece on that, and responded to several hundred comments over the years here. So there's not much new for me to say on that, other than the perception problems still exist.

I have been told by some commenters that investors in alternative ETFs, be they covered call or other types, are well aware that they do not hold up well when the market falls. I'm with them there, since we just saw this earlier this year.

That's a 6-month snapshot, just a moment in time, but one that reminds us that we now have a virtually unlimited number of ways to invest in and around the S&P 500 index. And frankly, many of them do not hold up to sustained scrutiny. Oh, they will work when the market recovers as fast as it fell, which is all we've seen since 2008. At some point, the drop will be followed by another drop, and another, and then finally, a period of volatility but no net movement in the major indexes.

I don't have a date when that will occur, but I'm quite prepared for when it does. Even it happens to be the day you read this article. Because managing risk at times like this is not about giving up gobs of upside. It is about HOW we proactively avoid big losses.

How to proactively avoid big losses (2025-26 version)

As you recall, 2008 was the year I became a mutual fund manager for the first time. I'm glad to say that I survived that well enough to get a similar gig some years later, before finally retiring from the business of running other people's money.

As I recall, my fund lost about 16% when the market was nearly cut in half. All I thought about then was the absolute return. So I was not accepting any pats on the back for beating the market by 30% in 7 months. My job was to manage risk. And relatively speaking, I did. But having $84 for every $100 I started with was no solace. Not to the child of a Dad who grew up during the Great Depression in NYC.

If I had been managing a "hedge fund," since most of them blew up around us back then, perhaps my performance would have been lauded, and we would have attracted capital. "Those guys who didn't lose half their fund or more." No such thing, ever. Money chases performance, then chases something else. But not relative return. It will be the same way this time around. Which is not good news for alternative ETFs that lose 80% or 90% of what the S&P 500 does. There will be a lot of them.

Why tell those old war stories? To remind us that a few things about 2025 are very different from back in 2008, when I received my "initiation" to the managed fund business. Most importantly, that the whole idea of "diversification" does not work when the market correlates. And now, more than ever, is what happens when the stock market sells off.

Above, I've gone way back to the proverbial well, to recall some of the things I hoped would plug the big hole in the boat of any portfolio I was associated with. "None of the above" was the best answer, as you can see.

The market started to recover at this point. But here's the issue. 2008 was a case of excesses built up over many years, some even stemming from the dot-com bubble that popped in 2000, and led to 3 straight down years for the S&P 500, the only time in history that has occurred.

Did anyone count on that? NO. Did anyone have a chance to proactively prepare for it? YES. Did they? Well, I can only account for one who did (me), much to the ridicule and/or distain of many who knew me. That included some clients, who felt like we were being too conservative with their assets.

My response: "you have enough money to live on for the rest of your life and at least a generation afterward ($2-5mm in investable assets was our client sweet spot). Some left us for "greener pastures" in 2007 and early 2008. Others agreed with the "avoid big loss" approach and joined us.

That chart above shows that 2008 was toward the end of a longer, secular market cycle. I prefer to look at such S&P 500 cycles as follows: take the peak (in prices and extreme sentiment), track the decline to its low...and then track the recovery to the old high.

That last part is the key. If I have $100 and it drops to $50, we all know I need to make 100% gain to make up for the 50% loss I just endured. But when I make that 100% gain, which in the period shown above took "only" about 3 years, through late 2011, all I did was earn back what I had lost.

Bottom line on that previous chart: from March 2000 through December 2011, the return of the S&P 500, including dividends, was zero. Let that soak in.

This is not some red flag-waving moment. It is just a reminder that markets are not rational, in either direction. Was the S&P 500's trailing P/E Ratio excessive at 35-45x on the left side of this chart? How about after the spike in 2008, when it dropped to 14x during 2011? Markets overshoot in both directions. This is a great time to keep that in mind.

2008 is ancient history. Or is it?

I've tried to this point to lay out some of my most relevant, vivid memories from the last time "buy the dips" didn't work. I do not know when investors will again lament that it did not occur again. But I know what I think is similar to, and better than (for DIY investors) than 17 years ago.

So I'll finish by explaining those and then showing a short list of "go-to's" for me. They are all ETFs. As my regular readers know, I use options to manage risk as well. I don't use single stocks. Because single stocks do not work when the market is being driven by a scenario in which strong liquidity turns to tight liquidity.

That, and the speculation that fed into it, are what is the downfall of every major stock market cycle. It will be the downfall of this current one too. When? When it is, that's when!

In other words, I do not spend my days guessing about the future. I plan for disaster always. In the same way I did not wait to turn 60 years old to buy life insurance. I became a father for the first time at age 32. I had insurance when it became that much more important to have it. Portfolio disaster protection is generally similar, just a very different type of decision. You don't want to wait until everyone knows there's a problem.

The routes to preserving capital are still there ("go to cash" etc.) but the best "bang for the buck" (put options on S&P 500 or other major market indexes, some of the ETFs I'll show) are best considered proactively. Not when you are reading articles about them every day. (Side prediction: the ETFs I mention below will be front page news...when the S&P 500 is down 20-30% or more).

2025 vs. 2008: what's the same?

1. Margin debt is off the charts. Leverage pops the bubble.

2. Everyone is speculating. ODTE options, those covered call ETFs, and much more.

3. FOMO and wealth envy are everywhere. There can be an entire article about this subject. Books, even. But suffice it to say, this is part of what drives the "chase" for higher asset prices, which works until it fails. Just ask the "Big Short" era folks.

2025 vs. 2008: what's different?

1. Many more ETFs that manage risk without sacrificing all return. I used Seeking Alpha's nicely-upgraded and easy to use ETF Screener. I asked it to show me the current funds in 8 different alternative categories. There were 482 of them. How many existed 10 years ago? Only 70 of them. How many were available back before 2007? 8 of them, all single inverse or leveraged inverse ETFs.

A list of my go-to's, which I use tactically in all market conditions, follows here. Note that these are NOT buy and hold funds. I may hold them weeks to months at most. My whole process is about assessing the level of major risk at any point in time, and applying a set of "defensive assets" via a combination of these ETFs. This is part of what I call "playing offense and defense at the same time."

In that chart above, I've run 6-month rolling returns of 4 of many I consider. There are 2 volatility ETFs (one that is more sensitive as it uses short term VIX futures, the other less so as it spread its VIX exposure out a few months). There's also ProShares Short S&P 500 ETF (SH), which is one of the "originals" I've used since before 2008. And ProShares UltraPro Short QQQ (SQQQ), part of a newer and expanding crop of leveraged inverse ETFs.

In short (pardon the pun), inverse-leveraged funds need to be used in small position size, and for short time frames. But as I see it, they cover a lot of ground with a small capital commitment, similar to a degree as put options do.

2. The best T-bill rates in a long time. Here is the past 24-year history of 6-month US Treasury Bills. They are off their peak level, but I see one of the highest levels this century. As I see it, any time I can get nearly 4% annualized to check out the scene for a half a year with some of my capital, it does 2 things: it pays me to wait, but it also provides an anchor, allowing me to be more aggressive and tactical with some of my other capital. The return of the 2 parts of my portfolio is the blended return and risk profile I want to have at any point in time.

3. Better access to information...just separate the help from the hype.

The great news is that investing has gone "mainstream." The bad news is that while we've never had more data and information at our fingertips, I dare say we've never used less of it. But that's an individual decision.

The bottom-line, is yours

Use that information, and use it with a skeptical eye, always. Be a self-directed investor. That means learning, analyzing and training to make decisions. Not necessarily trading decisions, since this is ultimately about investment allocation. And risk management.

This article was intended to set the scene for why and how I manage risk. Its essentially what I do for a living. Just for my own family now, after decades of doing it for other families. But without a doubt, there are enough similarities to 2008, and other very tough market cycles to take note. And not simply to blow them off, but to account for not only the possibility of what can happen, but virtually any scenario.

Final note for now: nothing I presented above has to mean long-term returns suffer. Not at all. If the S&P 500 rises continuously for another 5 years, sure. But more likely, the capital saved from adding proactive defense to one's offense puts an investor in great shape to capitalize on volatile markets, no matter what the trend is.
